unit.name: Inspecting hidden states and attention
unit.description: Shows how to request hidden states and attention tensors, how eval mode fits into analysis, and how to interpret layer/head visualizations.
unit.summary: This segment focuses on introspection rather than prediction. The speaker shows how to load a model with output_attentions=True and output_hidden_states=True so the forward pass returns internals that can be examined [ts=1491s] [ts=1508s]. He sets the model to eval mode and explains that eval mode is the standard PyTorch way to disable training-time behavior when the goal is only evaluation or analysis, making the process more efficient [ts=1520s] [ts=1610s]. The resulting output dictionary includes hidden states and attention weights; the speaker highlights the large tensor shapes and explains how to interpret them by layer, batch element, representation dimension, and query-key dimensions for attention [ts=1540s] [ts=1680s]. He then discusses plotting attentions across layers and heads, with rows corresponding to layers, columns corresponding to heads, and color intensity indicating attention magnitude, where yellow means larger values and dark colors are near zero [ts=1759s] [ts=1823s] [ts=1860s].

key_points.text: Setting output_attentions=True and output_hidden_states=True makes the model return internal activations for analysis.
model.eval() puts the model in evaluation mode and is the correct setting when inspecting a pre-trained model without training it.
Model outputs can include hidden_states and attentions in addition to logits.
Hidden-state shapes can be interpreted by layer, batch entry, and representation dimension.
In the attention visualization, rows correspond to layers and columns correspond to heads.
The heatmap color scale uses yellow for higher attention values and dark colors for values near zero.

unit_summary_for_generation: This segment focuses on introspection rather than prediction. The speaker shows how to load a model with output_attentions=True and output_hidden_states=True so the forward pass returns internals that can be examined [ts=1491s] [ts=1508s]. He sets the model to eval mode and explains that eval mode is the standard PyTorch way to disable training-time behavior when the goal is only evaluation or analysis, making the process more efficient [ts=1520s] [ts=1610s]. The resulting output dictionary includes hidden states and attention weights; the speaker highlights the large tensor shapes and explains how to interpret them by layer, batch element, representation dimension, and query-key dimensions for attention [ts=1540s] [ts=1680s]. He then discusses plotting attentions across layers and heads, with rows corresponding to layers, columns corresponding to heads, and color intensity indicating attention magnitude, where yellow means larger values and dark colors are near zero [ts=1759s] [ts=1823s] [ts=1860s].

kp_catalog.name: Model introspection with hidden states and attention
kp_catalog.description: Using Hugging Face model outputs to inspect layerwise hidden states and attention distributions for analysis and debugging.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because model introspection with hidden states and attention clarifies using Hugging Face model outputs to inspect layerwise hidden states and attention distributions for analysis and debugging, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Sequence classification outputs, loss, and predictions
kp_catalog.description: How to pass tokenized inputs through a classification model, read logits, obtain loss, and convert logits to predicted labels.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because sequence classification outputs, loss, and predictions clarifies how to pass tokenized inputs through a classification model, read logits, obtain loss, and convert logits to predicted labels, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
